concepts.title: Scalar Product
concepts.conceptBody:
Multiplication of Vectors

$\overrightarrow{\text{OL}},\text{ in vector }={{\left( \frac{\text{a}\cdot \text{b}}{|\text{a}{{|}^{2}}} \right)}^{{\vec{a}}}}$

Properties of Scalar product

(a) Commutative: i.e. a.b = b.a $=ab\cos \theta $

(b) Distributive: a. $(b+c)=a\cdot b+a\cdot c$

(c) If $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b}=0\quad \Rightarrow \quad |a||b|\cos \theta =0$ and $|a|,|b|\ne 0$

i.e. $\cos \theta =0\Rightarrow \theta =\pi /2$ or the two vectors are perpendicular to each other.

So for two perpendicular vectors, $a\cdot b=0$

(d) $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{a}=|a{{|}^{2}}$

as this can be written as $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{a}=|a||a|\cos 0$

(e) $(\text{ma})\cdot (\text{n}\overrightarrow{\text{b}})=(\text{mn})(\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{b}})$

(f) Orthonormal Trials

$\tilde{i},\tilde{j}~\And~\tilde{k}$ are known as orthonormal trials as all the 3 are normal to each other.

$\text{ so }\widetilde{\text{i}}\cdot \widetilde{\text{j}}=\widetilde{\text{j}}\cdot \widetilde{\text{k}}=\widetilde{\text{k}}\cdot \widetilde{\text{i}}=0$

$\widetilde{\text{i}}\cdot \widetilde{\text{i}}\text{ }=\widetilde{\text{j}}\cdot \widetilde{\text{j}}=\widetilde{\text{k}}\cdot \widetilde{\text{k}}=1$

(g) Angle between 2 vectors

if $\vec{a}={{a}_{1}}\tilde{i}+{{a}_{2}}\tilde{j}+{{a}_{3}}\tilde{k}$

$\vec{b}={{b}_{1}}\tilde{i}+{{b}_{2}}\tilde{j}+{{b}_{3}}\tilde{k}$

then $a\cdot b={{a}_{1}}{{b}_{1}}+{{a}_{2}}{{b}_{2}}+{{a}_{3}}{{b}_{3}}$

i.e. multiply the coefficients of same unit vectors $\tilde{i}$ with $\tilde{i}$, like this & angle between the vectors is given by

$\cos \theta \text{ }=\frac{{{a}_{1}}{{b}_{1}}+{{a}_{2}}{{b}_{2}}+{{a}_{3}}{{b}_{3}}}{|a||b|}$

$=\frac{{{a}_{1}}{{b}_{1}}+{{a}_{2}}{{b}_{2}}+{{a}_{3}}{{b}_{3}}}{\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+a_{3}^{2}}\sqrt{b_{1}^{2}+b_{2}^{2}+b_{3}^{2}}}$

concepts.title: questions on STP and its properties
concepts.conceptBody:
Triple products

Let $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ be three vectors. By increasing dot and cross between $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ in the same alphabetical order we introduce the following products:

$(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b})\cdot \vec{c},(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b})\times \vec{c},(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\cdot \vec{c}\,\,\,\text{and }(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\times \vec{c}$

Consider the product $(\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b}).\vec{c}$. Since $\vec{a}\cdot \vec{b}$ is a scalar quantity and dot product is defined between two vector quantities, therefore the product $(\vec{a}.\vec{b}).\vec{c}$ is not meaningful.

Similarly, $(\vec{a}.\vec{b})\times \vec{c}$ is not meaningful.

But $(\vec{a}\times \vec{b}).\vec{c}$ is meaningful, because $\vec{a}\times \vec{b}$ is a vector and its dot product with $\vec{c}$ i.e. $(\vec{a}\times \vec{b}).\vec{c}$ is a scalar quantity. This product is known as the scalar triple product of $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$.

The product $(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\times \vec{c}$ is also meaningful, because $\vec{a}\times \vec{b}$ is a vector and its cross-product with $\vec{c}$, i.e. $(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\times \vec{c}$ is also a vector. This product is known as the vector triple product of $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$.

concepts.title: Total Probability
concepts.conceptBody:
Theorem of Total Probability

To establish this result we start with the definition of a partition of a sample space.

A partition of a sample space

The collection of events $A_{1}, A_{2}, \ldots, A_{n}$ is said to partition a sample space $S$ if

(a) $A_{1} \cup A_{2} \cup \ldots \cup A_{n} = S$

(b) $A_{i} \cap A_{j} = \emptyset$ for all $i, j$

(c) $A_{i} \neq \emptyset$ for all $i$

In essence, a partition is a collection of non-empty, non-overlapping subsets of a sample space whose union is the sample space itself.

If $\text{B}$ is any event within $\text{S}$ then we can express $\text{B}$ as the union of subsets :

$\text{B}=\left( \text{B}\cap {{\text{A}}_{1}} \right)\cup \left( \text{B}\cap {{\text{A}}_{2}} \right)\cup \ldots \cup \left( \text{B}\cap {{\text{A}}_{\text{n}}} \right)$

The definition is illustrated in Figure in which an event $\text{B}$ in $\text{S}$ is represented by the shaded region.

The bracketed events $(B \cap A_{1})$, $(B \cap A_{2}), \ldots, (B \cap A_{n})$ are mutually exclusive (if one occurs then none of the others can occur) and so, using the addition law of probability for mutually exclusive events:

$\text{P}(B) = \text{P}(B \cap A_{1}) + \text{P}(B \cap A_{2}) + \ldots + \text{P}(B \cap A_{n})$

Each of the probabilities on the right-hand side may be expressed in terms of conditional probabilities:

$\text{P}(B \cap A_{i}) = \text{P}(B|A_{i}) \cdot \text{P}(A_{i})$

$\text{P}(B) = \text{P}(B|A_{1})\text{P}(A_{1}) + \text{P}(B|A_{2})\text{P}(A_{2}) + \ldots + \text{P}(B|A_{n})\text{P}(A_{n})$

$= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{P}(B|A_{i})\text{P}(A_{i})$

This is the theorem of Total Probability. A related theorem with many applications in statistics can be deduced from this, known as Bayes' theorem.

We solve the questions on total probability by tree diagram.

Here's the corrected text: So the total probability of event A is written as: $P(A) = P(E_1)P(A|E_1) + P(E_2)P(A|E_2) + \ldots + P(E_n)P(A|E_n)$ 

Q) A box contains N coins, m of which are fair and the rest are biased. The probability of getting a head when a fair coin is tossed is \( \frac{1}{2} \), while it is \( \frac{2}{3} \) when a biased coin is tossed. A coin is drawn from the box at random and is tossed twice. The first time it shows head and the second time it shows tail. What is the probability that the coin drawn is fair?

Solution: Let \( E_1 \) be the event that the coin drawn is fair and \( E_2 \) be the event that the coin drawn is biased.
Therefore, \( P(E_1) = \frac{m}{N} \) and \( P(E_2) = \frac{N - m}{N} \).
A is the event that on tossing the coin the head appears first and then appears tail.
Therefore, \( P(A) = P(E_1 \cap A) + P(E_2 \cap A) \) 
$= P(E_1)P(A|E_1) + P(E_2)P(A|E_2)$
$= \frac{m}{N}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{N-m}{N}\right)\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)$
$\text{We have to find the probability that A has happened because of } E_1$ Therefore, $P(E_1|A) = \frac{P(E_1 \cap A)}{P(A)}$
$= \frac{\frac{m}{N}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2}{\frac{m}{N}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{N-m}{N}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)}$
$= \frac{\frac{m}{4}}{\frac{m}{4} + \frac{2(N-m)}{9}} = \frac{9m}{m + 8N}$

concepts.title: Scalar Triple Product
concepts.conceptBody:
Scalar Triple Product

Definition: Let $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ be three vectors. Then the scalar $(\vec{a}\times \vec{b}).\vec{c}$ is called the scalar triple product of $\vec{a},\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$ and is denoted by $[\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]$.

Thus, $[\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]=(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\cdot \vec{c}$

Geometrical Interpretation of Scalar Triple Product

Let $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ be three vectors. Consider a parallelopiped having coterminous edges OA, OB and OC such that $\overrightarrow{\text{OA}}=\overrightarrow{\text{a}},\overrightarrow{\text{OB}}=\overrightarrow{\text{b}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{OC}}=\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$. Then $\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}$ is a vector perpendicular to the plane of $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$. Let $\phi $ be the angle between $\vec{c}$ and $\vec{a}\times \vec{b}$. If $\hat{n}$ is a unit vector along $\vec{a}\times \vec{b}$, then $\phi $ is also the angle between $\widehat{\text{n}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$.

Thus, the scalar triple product $[\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]$ represents the volume of the parallelopiped whose coterminous edges $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ from a right-handed system of vectors.

Properties Of Scalar Triple Product

1. If $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ are cyclically permuted the value of scalar triple product remains same.

i.e. $(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\cdot \vec{c}=(\vec{b}\times \vec{c})\cdot \vec{a}=(\vec{c}\times \vec{a})\cdot \vec{b}$
or $[\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]=[\vec{b}\vec{c}\vec{a}]=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
{\vec{c}} & {\vec{a}} & {\vec{b}} \\ \end{array} \right]$
2. The change of cyclic order of vector in scalar triple product changes the sign of the scalar triple product but not the magnitude.
i.e. $[\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]=-[\vec{b}\vec{a}\vec{c}]=-\left[ \left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {\vec{c}} & {\vec{b}} & {\vec{a}} \\ \end{array} \right]=-[\vec{a}\vec{c}\vec{b}] \right.$

3. In scalar triple product the position of dot and cross can be interchanged provided that the cyclic order of the vectors remains same i.e. $(\vec{a}\times \vec{b})\cdot \vec{c}=\vec{a}\cdot (\vec{b}\times \vec{c})$

4. The scalar triple product of three vectors is zero if any two of them are equal.

5. For any three vectors $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ and scalar $\lambda ,[\lambda \vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]=\lambda [\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]$.

6. The scalar triple product of three vectors is zero if any two of them are parallel or collinear.

7. If $\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}\vec{d}$ are four vectors, then $\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
\vec{a}+\vec{b} & {\vec{c}} & {\vec{d}} \\ \end{array} \right]=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {\vec{a}} & {\vec{c}} & {\vec{d}} \\ \end{array} \right]+\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {\vec{b}} & {\vec{c}} & {\vec{d}} \\ \end{array} \right]$

8. The necessary and sufficient condition for three non-zero, non-collinear vectors $\overrightarrow{\text{a}},\overrightarrow{\text{b}},\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ to be coplanar is $[\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]=0$ i.e., $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ are coplanar $\Leftrightarrow [\vec{a}\vec{b}\vec{c}]=0$.

9. For points with positive vectors $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$ and $\vec{d}$ will be coplanar if $[\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}]+[\vec{d},\vec{c},\vec{a}]+[\vec{d},\vec{a},\vec{b}]=[\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}]$

10. Distributivity of Cross-Product Over Vector addition

For any three vectors $\vec{a},\vec{b},\vec{c}$, we have

$\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}+\overrightarrow{\text{c}})=\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{b}}+\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}}.$

Scalar Triple Product in terms of components

Let $\text{ }\vec{a}={{a}_{1}}\hat{i}+{{a}_{2}}\hat{j}+{{a}_{3}}\hat{k},\vec{b}={{b}_{1}}\hat{i}+{{b}_{2}}\hat{j}+{{b}_{3}}\hat{k}\text{, and }\vec{c}={{c}_{1}}\hat{i}+{{c}_{2}}\hat{j}+{{c}_{3}}\hat{k}\text{ be three vectors}\text{. Then, we have }$
$[\overrightarrow{\text{a}}\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\overrightarrow{\text{c}}]=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{a}}_{1}} & {{\text{a}}_{2}} & {{\text{a}}_{3}} \\ ~{{\text{b}}_{1}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{2}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{3}} \\ {{\text{c}}_{1}} & {{\text{c}}_{2}} & {{\text{c}}_{3}} \\ \end{array} \right|.$

concepts.title: Geometry of Vectors
concepts.conceptBody:
Geometry of vectors

1. Angle bisector of $\mathbf{\vec{a} \And }\,\,\mathbf{\vec{b}}$

We know that diagonals of rhombus are angle bisectors also, so arrange in $\vec{a}\,\,\And \,\,\vec{b}$ to form a rhombus (remember vector are like free lines which can be moved parallel to their direction ratios, which we will cover in 3D)

 

2. Vector equation of a line:

The vector equation of a line passing through a point having position vector $\vec{a}$ and parallel to vector $\vec{b}$ is $\overrightarrow{\text{r}}=\overrightarrow{\text{a}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{\text{b}}$, where $\lambda $ is scalar.

3. Vector equation of a plane:

The vector equation of a plane passing through a point having position vector $\vec{a}$ andcontaining vectors $\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\,\,\And \,\,\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ is

$\overrightarrow{\text{r}}=\overrightarrow{\text{a}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{\text{b}}+\mu \overrightarrow{\text{c}}\quad ,\quad \text{ where }\lambda ,\mu \in \text{R}$

OR

Let $r$ be a point on the required plane with a given point $\vec{a}$ and normal to the plane $\vec{n}$. Then equation of plane becomes

$(\overrightarrow{\text{r}}-\overrightarrow{\text{a}})\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{n}}=0$

For three points 

The vector equation of a plane passing through 3 points having position vectors $\overrightarrow{\text{a}},\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }\overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ is

1. $\overrightarrow{\text{r}}=(1-\lambda -\mu )\overrightarrow{\text{a}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{\text{b}}+\mu \overrightarrow{\text{c}}$

2. $(\overrightarrow{\text{r}}-\overrightarrow{\text{a}})\cdot (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}})=0$

The reasoning for second one is easy. See we needed a normal vector to the plane and we know that cross product produces a vector normal to both the vectors, so the resultant of $\overrightarrow{\text{b}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ is perpendicular to the plane.

Bisectors of the angles between 2 lines:

1. The angle bisectors of the line $\overrightarrow{\text{r}}=\lambda \overrightarrow{\text{a}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\text{r}}=\mu \overrightarrow{\text{b}}$ are given by

$\overrightarrow{\text{r}}={{\lambda }^{\prime }}\left( \frac{\overrightarrow{\text{a}}}{|\overrightarrow{\text{a}}|}+\frac{\overrightarrow{\text{b}}}{|\overrightarrow{\text{b}}|} \right)$

2. The angle bisector for lines $\overrightarrow{\text{r}}=\overrightarrow{\text{a}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{\text{b}}\quad \overrightarrow{\text{r}}=\overrightarrow{\text{a}}+\mu \overrightarrow{\text{c}}$ \{i.e. lines originating or passing through the point a) is given by

$\overrightarrow{\text{r}}=\overrightarrow{\text{a}}+{{\lambda }^{\prime }}\left( \frac{\overrightarrow{\text{b}}}{|\text{b}|}+\frac{\overrightarrow{\text{c}}}{|\text{c}|} \right).$

Equation of lines through 2 points $\vec{a}\text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }\vec{b}$

It is given by $\overrightarrow{\text{r}}=\overrightarrow{\text{a}}+\lambda (\overrightarrow{\text{b}}-\overrightarrow{\text{a}})$

Logic:

It is easy, as we need a parallel vector along the line & $(\vec{b}-\vec{a})$ will serve that purpose now.

concepts.title: Tetrahedron
concepts.conceptBody:
Tetrahedron

In the figure pull the point $\text{O}$ out of plane. The 3D figure which you can imagine now is tetrahedron.

Properties of tetrahedron 

1. If $\overline{\text{OA}}=\overline{\text{a}},\overline{\text{OB}}=\overline{\text{b}}\,\,\And \,\,\overline{\text{OC}}=\overline{\text{c}}$, then volume of tetrahedron $=\frac{1}{6}[\overline{\text{a}}\overline{\text{b}}\overline{\text{c}}]$

2. The angle between any two plane faces of regular tetrahedron is ${{\cos }^{-1}}\frac{1}{3}$

3. Any two edges of regular tetrahedron are perpendicular to each other

4. Angle between any edge & a face not containing the angle is ${{\cos }^{-1}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}}$, for any regular tetrahedron
